summary: This notebook implements a highly optimized inference pipeline for a specialized machine translation task (Akkadian to English) using a pre-trained Seq2Seq model. It demonstrates advanced decoding strategies, specifically Minimum Bayes Risk (MBR) selection, by combining beam search and sampling candidates to maximize average chrF++ similarity. The code showcases performance optimizations like BF16-only automatic mixed precision, Hugging Face's BetterTransformer, bucket batching, and aggressive vectorized post-processing to handle translation artifacts. Readers should take away practical techniques for maximizing inference speed and output quality in low-resource or specialized translation competitions without retraining.
competition_flow: Loads a CSV of Akkadian transliterations, applies regex-based text cleaning, runs inference with a pre-trained Seq2Seq model using MBR decoding and aggressive post-processing, and saves the final English translations to a submission CSV.
data_reading: Reads test data from a CSV file using pandas.read_csv with UTF-8 encoding, expecting 'id' and 'transliteration' columns.
data_processing: Vectorized regex-based text cleaning replaces ellipses/gaps with <big_gap> and <gap> tokens.

Aggressive post-processing normalizes whitespace, removes forbidden characters, fixes punctuation spacing, merges adjacent gaps, removes annotations, converts fractions, and deduplicates repeated words/n-grams.
Uses bucket batching for DataLoader to minimize padding overhead.
ensembling: Implements Minimum Bayes Risk (MBR) decoding by generating candidates via beam search and sampling, then selecting the output that maximizes the average chrF++ similarity to all other candidates in the pool.
insights: MBR decoding with a mix of beam search and sampling candidates can significantly improve translation quality over standard greedy or beam search alone.
Aggressive, vectorized post-processing is crucial for cleaning translation artifacts like repeated words, forbidden characters, and spacing issues in specialized domains.
BF16-only automatic mixed precision with fallback to FP32 provides a safe and effective way to accelerate inference on compatible GPUs without numerical instability.
Bucket batching and adaptive beam sizing based on input length can optimize memory usage and inference speed.
